one_line_takeaway: By embedding a keyed, secretly-directioned watermark into an LLM's own hidden activations during fine-tuning (rather than relying on an external, statically-known guard classifier), the provider gets a monitor that adaptive jailbreak attackers cannot reliably evade without knowledge of the secret key, at a modest and mostly math-reasoning-concentrated utility cost.
key_finding: Existing external guard models are shown to be evadable by adaptive attackers: at 1% FPR, ASR (fraction of harmful-but-successful generations that evade the monitor) reaches 0.87-0.90 for AutoDAN against LlamaGuard (0.8988) and QwenGuard (0.8750), and LlamaGuard's AUROC collapses to 0.4092 on AutoDAN and 0.4353 on Multilingual. Activation watermarking (ActWM) achieves the highest AUROC of any method on every one of the four jailbreak attack families: 0.9292 (Jailbroken), 0.9229 (DeepInception), 0.9619 (Multilingual), and 0.9048 (AutoDAN), versus QwenGuard's best of 0.9261/0.8697/0.9227/0.7851 respectively on those same attacks -- i.e. up to a ~52% relative reduction in evasion versus guard baselines under adaptive attack, per the abstract's headline number (not independently re-derived from the ASR table by this reviewer, since the abstract's '52%' figure is not explicitly recomputed in the body text tables shown). On in-distribution BeaverTails, ActWM (0.8779 AUROC) is roughly competitive with QwenGuard (0.8489) rather than dominant. Utility cost ('alignment tax'): under 2 percentage points change on BBH (+0.95pp), IFEval (-1.96pp), MMLU-pro (+1.41pp), TruthfulQA (-0.59pp), with larger drops on math reasoning: GSM8K -7.13pp and MATH-Hard -2.64pp. Cross-model transfer attacks (Mistral-7B attacker vs Qwen2.5-7B defender) yield low ASR@1%FPR (0.46%-13.21% across attack types) and AUROC 0.897-0.955, indicating limited attack transfer across base models. The key-dependence experiment shows attacks crafted against one secret key show reduced but non-zero evasion against detectors instantiated with a different key (off-diagonal evasion rates below the matched-key diagonal in the 4x4 confusion matrix, e.g. diagonal ~1.00 vs off-diagonal 0.24-0.59). The secret-extraction game over 20 synthetic entities achieves ~80% diagonal attribution accuracy (vs 5% random chance) with a macro AUC of 0.805, and at FPR=1e-4 the deployment-style detector retains ~80% mean TPR per entity.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'activation watermarking', a defense for LLM safety monitoring that fine-tunes a base LLM so that a secret, randomly-sampled Gaussian direction w_l in each target layer's hidden-state space becomes aligned with activations produced during policy-violating (harmful) generations, and orthogonal to activations during benign generations. A KL-divergence term to a frozen reference model constrains drift, and a linear ramp weighting concentrates the watermark loss on tokens after a labeled 'harmful onset offset' so the signal doesn't fire on benign scaffolding. At inference, the provider computes a cosine-similarity statistic T_k(pi,x) averaged over assistant tokens and flags a response if it exceeds a calibrated threshold tau_k. The paper also formalizes an adaptive-attacker threat model/security game (attacker knows the monitoring algorithm but not secret key k, can query unboundedly) and proposes two concrete adaptive-attack families (template-based rejection sampling using Jailbroken/DeepInception/Multilingual templates, and an optimization-based DPO/AutoDAN-style prompt-rewriter) to stress-test both existing guard models and their own defense.
threat_model: Provider deploys LLM M with a black-box detector D_k(pi,x) parameterized by secret key k not revealed to users. Attacker has black-box API access only (no gradients/activations of the deployed model), can issue unbounded queries, knows the monitoring algorithm's design/architecture but not the secret key k. Attacker builds a surrogate model M_k' with a self-chosen key k' (or an entirely different base model, e.g. Mistral-7B-Instruct, for transfer attacks), optimizes jailbreak prompts against the surrogate/detector pair, then transfers the resulting prompts to the real deployment. Attacker goal: elicit at least one harmful response from the true model while evading D_k on every query. The paper explicitly excludes white-box adversaries (no gradient/activation access) as a stated limitation, and notes the security argument is empirical, not provably robust.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) Black-box assumption -- the threat model assumes attackers cannot access gradients or activations of the deployed model, excluding white-box adversaries; realistic for API-only deployment but not a complete threat coverage. (2) No provable guarantees -- security is argued empirically against concrete adaptive attacks the authors constructed; they explicitly state stronger, unconsidered adaptive strategies could reduce detection rates, and provable robustness bounds remain open. (3) Automated/proxy labels -- harmfulness judgments in evaluation rely on a GPT oracle and Qwen Stream Guard, so reported metrics may inherit those upstream models' blind spots or biases. (4) Utility evaluation limited to public academic benchmarks (BBH, IFEval, MMLU-pro, TruthfulQA, GSM8K, MATH-Hard) and does not capture real-world usage distribution at scale; watermarking requires directly modifying/fine-tuning the base LLM, with GSM8K/MATH-Hard showing the largest utility regressions (up to -7.1pp), which the authors frame as an acceptable but real trade-off.

datasets_used: BeaverTails (Ji et al. 2023) -- training and held-out evaluation of harmful/benign prompt-response pairs
XSTest (Rottger et al. 2023) -- source of harmful seed prompts for adversarial variant generation
EasyJailbreak framework (Zhou et al. 2024) -- used to generate adversarial variants via Jailbroken, DeepInception, Multilingual, and AutoDAN attack strategies
BBH, IFEval, MMLU-pro, TruthfulQA, GSM8K, MATH-Hard -- standard capability/utility benchmarks used to measure the alignment/utility tax of watermarking
quantitative_results: AUROC (ActWM vs best guard baseline) per attack: BeaverTails 0.8779 vs QwenGuard 0.8489; Jailbroken 0.9292 vs QwenGuard 0.9261; DeepInception 0.9229 vs ActProbe 0.8711; Multilingual 0.9619 vs ActProbe 0.9484; AutoDAN 0.9048 vs ActProbe 0.8746
ASR@1%FPR (lower is better) for AutoDAN: Base 1.0000, LlamaGuard 0.8988, QwenGuard 0.8750, ActProbe 0.7532, ActWM 0.6786
Utility deltas (base -> watermarked): BBH +0.95pp, IFEval -1.96pp, MMLU-pro +1.41pp, TruthfulQA -0.59pp, GSM8K -7.13pp, MATH-Hard -2.64pp

Cross-model transfer attack (Mistral-7B attacker -> Qwen2.5-7B ActWM defender): AUROC 0.9501 (Jailbroken), 0.9545 (DeepInception), 0.8972 (Multilingual), 0.9319 (AutoDAN); ASR@1%FPR 4.60% (Jailbroken), 0.24% (DeepInception), 13.21% (Multilingual), 0.50% (AutoDAN)
Secret-extraction game (N=20 synthetic entities): ~80% diagonal attribution accuracy vs 5% random chance, macro AUC=0.805, mean TPR ~80% at FPR=1e-4

Model-size generalization (7B vs 14B ActWM), AUROC: Jailbroken 0.9329 vs 0.9146; DeepInception 0.9082 vs 0.9840; Multilingual 0.9541 vs 0.9370; AutoDAN 0.8866 vs 0.8905; IFEval utility 0.5804 (7B) vs 0.8194 (14B) vs 0.8244 (14B base)
Threshold calibration AUROC range across attacks: 0.905 (AutoDAN) to 0.962 (Multilingual); TPR@1%FPR most favorable for Multilingual (0.738), least favorable for DeepInception (0.211)
baselines_compared: ActProbe -- internal activation-probe baseline following the intuition of Jiang et al. 2025 (HiddenDetect)
Base model with no monitor (upper bound on ASR)
